nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs, rubble, and rocky areas at depths of 1–30 meters

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding largely on coral polyps, algae, and small invertebrates. In captivity, tends to accept a varied diet of mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, spirulina, marine algae, nori, and seaweed. Smaller or newly acquired specimens have been known to be reluctant feeders; acquiring medium-to-large individuals (>7 cm) is generally advised to improve acceptance of prepared foods.

avoidWith: other butterflyfishes with similar patterns (in smaller tanks)

summary: A peaceful Indo-Pacific butterflyfish with distinctive diagonal black chevron markings, generally considered suitable for intermediate aquarists in well-established reef-adjacent systems. Not generally considered reef-safe due to a tendency to nip coral polyps and invertebrates.
careNotes:
The Indian Vagabond Butterflyfish (Chaetodon decussatus) is native to the Indo-West Pacific, ranging from the Maldives and India through Sri Lanka and the Andaman Sea to the western Indo-Malayan Archipelago. Adults are generally found in pairs on rich coral reefs and rocky areas down to about 30 meters; juveniles tend to be solitary.

This species is generally considered not reef-safe, as it has been documented consuming coral polyps (particularly soft and LPS corals), tubeworms, and occasionally nipping at Tridacna clam mantles. One minority source rated it as reef-safe, but the majority of sources and field observations indicate otherwise.

Feeding can be a challenge, particularly with smaller or recently wild-caught specimens. It is generally advised to source medium-sized individuals (>7 cm) to improve the likelihood of transitioning to prepared foods. A varied diet including meaty items, marine algae, and vitamin supplements is recommended.

Water temperature recommendations vary across sources: LiveAquaria suggests 22–26°C, FishBase records the wild range as approximately 25–29°C, and other hobbyist sources indicate 24–28°C. A mid-range of 24–28°C has been used here as a reasonable average; the true comfortable range is likely broad.

Minimum tank size recommendations similarly varied widely, from 150 L (40 gal) to 500 L (132 gal); sources with more detailed care notes tend to emphasize the species' need for ample open swimming space and well-established systems. A minimum of 300 L (approximately 80 gal) has been used here as a cautious, rounded estimate.

Conspecific aggression is generally low when multiple individuals are introduced simultaneously; in smaller tanks, aggression toward other butterflyfishes with similar patterning has been reported. This species is typically kept as a mated pair in the aquarium hobby.

Captive breeding has not been documented; the species is commercially harvested from the wild for the aquarium trade.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding documentation found. Adults are oviparous and form pairs in the wild. Captive reproduction is generally considered not achievable under typical aquarium conditions.

additional.name: FishBase (fishbase.se)
additional.notes: Used as secondary after fishbase.org (summary/10352) returned HTTP 403. Confirmed adult size (20 cm TL), native range (Indo-West Pacific: Maldives, India, Sri Lanka, Andaman Sea), temperature range (25.4–29.2°C), habitat (rich coral reefs and rocky areas 1–30 m), family Chaetodontidae, order Acanthuriformes. Pairs in adults, juveniles solitary.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (20 cm), tank size recommendation (500 L), reef safety (not reef safe), diet requirements, and temperament. Minimum tank size of 500 L noted but considered conservative; averaged with other sources to arrive at 300 L.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), temperament (peaceful), and grouping (pair). This source rated reef safety as 'yes', contrary to LiveAquaria and ReefApp; majority view of not-reef-safe adopted.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documentation found. Oviparous; forms breeding pairs in the wild. Not known to reproduce in typical aquarium settings.
